summary: Gruber analyzes Facebook's launch of Instant Articles, a feature that renders publisher content natively within the Facebook app rather than loading mobile web pages. He praises the speed and design quality, noting it's built by the Facebook Paper team, and sees it as further evidence that native mobile code is making the browser-based web look obsolete even for article publishing. He highlights the convenience advantage over standalone publisher apps since users already check Facebook constantly. He notes Facebook is treating iPhone, not generic 'mobile,' as their first-class platform. While skeptical about publishers ceding control to Facebook, he acknowledges the business terms are surprisingly favorable.
key_points: Instant Articles loads content up to 10x faster than mobile web, and Gruber sees native code displacing the browser even for simple article publishing
The convenience of encountering articles within an app people already check constantly gives Facebook a structural advantage over standalone publisher apps and aggregators like Flipboard
Facebook is treating iPhone specifically — not mobile generically — as their first-class platform, with no Android support announced
The business terms are surprisingly publisher-friendly: keep 100% of revenue from your own ads, or take 70% if Facebook sells them, with no exclusivity requirement
Gruber worries the slowness of the mobile web and over-produced web design trends could hurt even lean sites like Daring Fireball, since the pages he links to load slowly
money_quotes: Not only is native mobile code winning for app development, but with things like Instant Articles, native is making the browser-based web look like a relic even just for publishing articles.
I worry that the inherent slowness of the web and ill-considered trend toward over-produced web design is going to start hurting traffic to DF.
People just don't check many apps — check the New York Times, check National Geographic, check BBC News — that just isn't how people use their phones.
There are only so many apps people will check for "new stuff" every day.
For the moment at least, Facebook isn't really treating "mobile" as their first-class target platform — they're treating the iPhone as their first-class target platform.
Seems dangerous to cede control over your content to a company like Facebook.
key_insight: Facebook Instant Articles represents native mobile code's encroachment on the browser-based web's last stronghold — simple article publishing — and its convenience advantage over both mobile web and standalone apps may be irresistible despite the risks of ceding control to Facebook.
